subject: Physics
q:
A prism of angle $75^{\circ}$ and refractive index $\sqrt{3}$ is coated with thin film of refractive index 1.5 only at the back exit surface. To have total internal reflection at the back exit surface the incident angle must be $\_\_\_\_$
($\sin 15^{\circ}=0.25$ and $\sin 25^{\circ}=0.43$)
question:
A prism of angle $75^{\circ}$ and refractive index $\sqrt{3}$ is coated with thin film of refractive index 1.5 only at the back exit surface. To have total internal reflection at the back exit surface the incident angle must be $\_\_\_\_$
($\sin 15^{\circ}=0.25$ and $\sin 25^{\circ}=0.43$)
solution:
$r_1 + r_2 = 75^{\circ}$
For T I R at back surface
$\sqrt{3} \sin r_2 = \dfrac{3}{2} \sin 90^{\circ}$
$r_2 \geq 60^{\circ}$
$r_1 \leq 15^{\circ}$
$1 \cdot \sin i = \sqrt{3} \sin 15^{\circ}$
$\sin i = 1.73 \times 0.25$
$\sin i = 0.433$
$i = 25^{\circ} \Rightarrow i < 25^{\circ}$

subject: Physics
q: A ray of light passes from a denser medium to a rarer medium at an angle of incidence i. The reflected and refracted rays make an angle of 90° with each other. The angle of reflection and refraction are respectively r and r'. The critical angle is given by,
question: A ray of light passes from a denser medium to a rarer medium at an angle of incidence i. The reflected and refracted rays make an angle of 90° with each other. The angle of reflection and refraction are respectively r and r'. The critical angle is given by,
solution:
r+r'+90°=180°⇒r'=90-r=90-i

n1sini=n2sinr'=n2sin(90-i)
n1sini=n2cosi⇒tani=n2n1
Now sinC=n2n1=tani
⇒C=sin-1(tani)=sin-1(tanr)

subject: Physics
q: A thin prism of angle 6° and refractive index for yellow light nY1.5 is combined with another prism of angle 5° and nY=1.55. The combination produces no dispersion. The net average deviation δ produced by the combination is 1x°. The value of x is _____ .
question: A thin prism of angle 6° and refractive index for yellow light nY1.5 is combined with another prism of angle 5° and nY=1.55. The combination produces no dispersion. The net average deviation δ produced by the combination is 1x°. The value of x is _____ .
solution:
The formula for deviation due to a thin prism is given by, δ=Aμ-1.

Therefore, for prism 1:

δ1=61.5-1=3°

And for prism 2:

δ2=-51.55-1=-2.75°

⇒δnet=δ1+δ2=3°-2.75°=0.25°=14°

Therefore, x=4.

The angle made by the emergent ray with second face of prism will be:
solution:
Incidence angle on one face of a prism, i=60°. Angle of the prism, A=30°,

the mergent ray that makes an angle 30° with incident ray. We know, deviation angle, δ= Angle between the incident ray and emergent ray=30°

⇒δ=i+e-A
⇒30°=60°+e−30∘
⇒e=0°. So, the angle with the face, =90° .

subject: Physics
q: A plane convex lens of refractive index $1.5$ and radius of curvature $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ is silvered at the curved surface. Now this lens has been used to form the image of an object. At what distance from this lens an object be placed in order to have a real image of the size of the object?
question: A plane convex lens of refractive index $1.5$ and radius of curvature $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ is silvered at the curved surface. Now this lens has been used to form the image of an object. At what distance from this lens an object be placed in order to have a real image of the size of the object?
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{F}=\frac{2}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_m} \\
& \text { and } \frac{1}{f_1}=(1.5-1)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-30}\right)=\frac{1}{60} \\
& \text { and } f_m=15 \mathrm{~cm} . \\
& \therefore F=10 \mathrm{~cm} .
\end{aligned}
$$
Object should be placed at $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ from the lens.

q: The refracting angle of a prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and refractive index of the material of the prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cot</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>. Then the angle of minimum deviation will be
question: The refracting angle of a prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and refractive index of the material of the prism is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cot</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math>. Then the angle of minimum deviation will be
solution:
As we know, angle of minimum deviation is given by, ⇒μ=sinδm+A2sinA2

⇒cotA2=sinδm+A2sinA2

⇒cosA2sinA2=sinδm+A2sinA2

⇒sin90°-A2=sinδm+A2

Therefore, 90°-A2=δm+A2  ⇒δm=180°-2A

2 R t = t 2 + r 2

R = t 2 + r 2 2 t = 0 · 3 2 + 3 2 2 × 0 · 3

Use 1 f = μ - 1 1 R 1 - 1 R 2

1 f = 1 . 5 - 1 1 1 5 = 1 3 0

f=30 cm

subject: Physics
q:
As shown in the diagram, when the incident ray is parallel to base of the prism, the emergent ray grazes along the second surface.

If refractive index of the material of prism is $\sqrt{2}$, the angle $\theta$ of prism is.
question:
As shown in the diagram, when the incident ray is parallel to base of the prism, the emergent ray grazes along the second surface.

If refractive index of the material of prism is $\sqrt{2}$, the angle $\theta$ of prism is.
solution:
From the geometry of the prism, the angle of the prism is $A = 180^\circ - (45^\circ + \theta)$.
The incident ray is parallel to the base, so the angle of incidence $i$ at the first surface is equal to the base angle $45^\circ$. Thus, $i = 45^\circ$.
Applying Snell's law at the first surface: $1 \cdot \sin(45^\circ) = \mu \sin(r_1)$.
Given $\mu = \sqrt{2}$, we have $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \sin(r_1) \implies \sin(r_1) = \frac{1}{2} \implies r_1 = 30^\circ$.
At the second surface, the emergent ray grazes the surface, so the angle of emergence $e = 90^\circ$.
Applying Snell's law at the second surface: $\mu \sin(r_2) = 1 \cdot \sin(90^\circ)$.
$\sqrt{2} \sin(r_2) = 1 \implies \sin(r_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \implies r_2 = 45^\circ$.
The relation between prism angle and internal angles is $A = r_1 + r_2$.
$A = 30^\circ + 45^\circ = 75^\circ$.
Substituting $A$ in the first equation: $75^\circ = 180^\circ - (45^\circ + \theta)$.
$75^\circ = 135^\circ - \theta \implies \theta = 135^\circ - 75^\circ = 60^\circ$.

subject: Physics
q:
A container contains a liquid with refractive index of 1.2 up to a height of 60 cm and another liquid having refractive index 1.6 is added to height H above first liquid. If viewed from above, the apparent shift in the position of bottom of container is 40 cm. The value of H is ______ cm.
(Consider liquids are immisible)
question:
A container contains a liquid with refractive index of 1.2 up to a height of 60 cm and another liquid having refractive index 1.6 is added to height H above first liquid. If viewed from above, the apparent shift in the position of bottom of container is 40 cm. The value of H is ______ cm.
(Consider liquids are immisible)
solution:
$y=$ apparent depth of bottom
$\frac{\mathrm{y}}{1}=\frac{\mathrm{H}}{1.6}+\frac{60}{1.2}$
Shift $=40$
$H+60-y=40$
$\mathrm{H}+60-\frac{\mathrm{H}}{1.6}-\frac{60}{1.2}=40$
$\frac{6}{16} \mathrm{H}=30$
$\mathrm{H}=80 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: The refractive index of the material of a concave lens is $\mu$. It is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$. A parallel beam of light is incident on the lens. The path of the emergent rays when $\mu_1>\mu$ is:
question: The refractive index of the material of a concave lens is $\mu$. It is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$. A parallel beam of light is incident on the lens. The path of the emergent rays when $\mu_1>\mu$ is:
solution:
If a lens of refractive index $\mu$ is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$, then its focal length in medium is given by
$$
\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}}=\left({ }_{\mathrm{m}} \mu_l-1\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}\right)-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)
$$
If $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is the focal length of lens in air, then
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\left({ }_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_l-1\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)\right. \\
&\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\frac{\left({ }_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_l-1\right)}{\left({ }_{\mathrm{m}} \mu_l-1\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$
If $\mu_1>\mu$, then $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}$ and $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}$ have opposite signs and the nature of lens changes i.e. a convex lens diverges the light rays and concave lens converges the light rays. Thus given option (a) is correct.

subject: Physics
q: A compound microscope is designed with two symmetric biconvex lenses. The objective lens is cut vertically, creating two identical plano-convex lenses. One of them is used in place of original objective lens. To retain same magnification keeping the object distance unchanged, the tube length has to be
question: A compound microscope is designed with two symmetric biconvex lenses. The objective lens is cut vertically, creating two identical plano-convex lenses. One of them is used in place of original objective lens. To retain same magnification keeping the object distance unchanged, the tube length has to be
solution:
Focal length of the objective after cutting:
For a symmetric biconvex lens with both radii of magnitude $R$:
$\dfrac{1}{f_o} = (\mu - 1)\left(\dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{-R}\right) = \dfrac{2(\mu - 1)}{R}$

When the lens is cut vertically (perpendicular to the principal axis), each half becomes a plano-convex lens with one flat surface:
$\dfrac{1}{f_o'} = (\mu - 1)\left(\dfrac{1}{R} - 0\right) = \dfrac{\mu - 1}{R}$

Comparing the two: $f_o' = 2f_o$, so the focal length of the objective doubles.

Magnification of a compound microscope:
$M \approx \dfrac{L}{f_o} \cdot \dfrac{D}{f_e}$

where $L$ is the tube length, $f_o$ is the objective focal length, $f_e$ is the eyepiece focal length and $D$ is the least distance of distinct vision.

Since the eyepiece and $D$ are unchanged, equating the initial and final magnifications:
$\dfrac{L_{\text{initial}}}{f_o} \cdot \dfrac{D}{f_e} = \dfrac{L_{\text{final}}}{2f_o} \cdot \dfrac{D}{f_e}$

$\dfrac{L_{\text{initial}}}{f_o} = \dfrac{L_{\text{final}}}{2f_o}$

$L_{\text{final}} = 2\,L_{\text{initial}}$

Hence, to maintain the same magnification, the tube length must be doubled.

The correct option is $(1)$ increased two times.

subject: Physics
q: The refractive index of the material of a glass prism is $\sqrt{3}$. The angle of minimum deviation is equal to the angle of the prism. What is the angle of the prism?
question: The refractive index of the material of a glass prism is $\sqrt{3}$. The angle of minimum deviation is equal to the angle of the prism. What is the angle of the prism?
solution:
$\mu=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{A+\delta_{\min }}{2}\right)}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}$

Given $\delta_{\min }=\mathrm{A}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \sqrt{3}=\frac{\sin A}{\sin \frac{A}{2}}=\frac{2 \sin \frac{A}{2} \cos \frac{A}{2}}{\sin \frac{A}{2}} \\
& \cos \frac{A}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\
& A=60^{\circ}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
An initially parallel cylindrical beam travels in a medium of refractive index $\mu(I)=\mu_0+\mu_2 I$, where $\mu_0$ and $\mu_2$ are positive constants and $\mathrm{I}$ is the intensity of the light beam. The intensity of the beam is decreasing with increasing radius.
As the beam enters the medium, it will
question:
An initially parallel cylindrical beam travels in a medium of refractive index $\mu(I)=\mu_0+\mu_2 I$, where $\mu_0$ and $\mu_2$ are positive constants and $\mathrm{I}$ is the intensity of the light beam. The intensity of the beam is decreasing with increasing radius.
As the beam enters the medium, it will
options: diverge near the axis and converge near the periphery
solution:
As intensity is maximum at axis,
$\therefore \quad \mu$ will be maximum and speed will be minimum on the axis of the beam.
$\therefore \quad$ beam will converge.

subject: Physics
q: A thin prism with angle $5^{\circ}$ of refractive index 1.72 is combined with another prism of refractive index 1.9 to produce dispersion without deviation. The angle of second prism is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A thin prism with angle $5^{\circ}$ of refractive index 1.72 is combined with another prism of refractive index 1.9 to produce dispersion without deviation. The angle of second prism is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For dispersion without deviation, the condition is $(\mu_1 - 1)A_1 + (\mu_2 - 1)A_2 = 0$.
Substituting values: $(1.72 - 1) \times 5 + (1.9 - 1) \times A_2 = 0$
$0.72 \times 5 + 0.9 \times A_2 = 0$
$3.6 + 0.9 A_2 = 0$
$A_2 = -4°$
The magnitude of the second prism angle is 4°.

subject: Physics
q: Calculate the limit of resolution of a telescope objective having a diameter of <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , if it has to detect light of wavelength <math><mn>500</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> coming from a star.
question: Calculate the limit of resolution of a telescope objective having a diameter of <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , if it has to detect light of wavelength <math><mn>500</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> coming from a star.
solution:
Limit of resolution of the telescope =1.22λD
θ=1.22×500×10-9200×10-2=305×10-9 radian

subject: Physics
q: A convex lens is made from glass material having refractive index of $1.4$ with same radius of curvature on both sides. The ratio of its focal length and radius of curvature is ______.
question: A convex lens is made from glass material having refractive index of $1.4$ with same radius of curvature on both sides. The ratio of its focal length and radius of curvature is ______.
solution:
Using the lens maker's formula:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = (\mu - 1) \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2} \right)$

For an equiconvex lens, $R_1 = R$ and $R_2 = -R$. Given $\mu = 1.4$:

$\dfrac{1}{f} = (1.4 - 1) \left( \dfrac{1}{R} - \dfrac{1}{-R} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{f} = 0.4 \times \dfrac{2}{R}$

$\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{0.8}{R}$

$\dfrac{f}{R} = \dfrac{1}{0.8} = 1.25$

Answer: $1.25$

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement-1: Short wave transmission is achieved due to the total internal reflection of the e-m wave from an appropriate height in the ionosphere.
Statement-2: Refractive index of a plasma is independent of the frequency of e-m waves.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement-1: Short wave transmission is achieved due to the total internal reflection of the e-m wave from an appropriate height in the ionosphere.
Statement-2: Refractive index of a plasma is independent of the frequency of e-m waves.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is false, Statement- 2 is true.
Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is true but Statement $-2$ is not the correct explanation of statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true and Statement $-2$ is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
solution:
Effective refractive index of the ionosphere
$$
\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{eff}}=\mathrm{n}_0\left[1-\frac{80.5 \mathrm{~N}}{\mathrm{f}^2}\right]^{1 / 2}
$$
where $\mathrm{f}$ is the frequency of em waves

subject: Physics
q: The speed of light in media 'A' and 'B' are 2.0×1010 cm s-1 and 1.5×1010 cm s-1 respectively. A ray of light enters from the medium B to A at an incident angle'θ'. If the ray suffers total internal reflection, then
question: The speed of light in media 'A' and 'B' are 2.0×1010 cm s-1 and 1.5×1010 cm s-1 respectively. A ray of light enters from the medium B to A at an incident angle'θ'. If the ray suffers total internal reflection, then
solution:
Given:vA=2×108 m s-1 and   vB=1.5×108 m s-1

For critical angle:sinθc=μAμB=cvA×vBc=1.5×1082×108=34

⇒θc=sin-134

Now for TIR, θ>θC

So, θc>sin-134

subject: Physics
q: In a microscope of tube length 10 cm two convex lenses are arranged with focal length of 2 cm and 5 cm. Total magnification obtained with this system for normal adjustment is $(5)^{k}$. The value of $k$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: In a microscope of tube length 10 cm two convex lenses are arranged with focal length of 2 cm and 5 cm. Total magnification obtained with this system for normal adjustment is $(5)^{k}$. The value of $k$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
For a microscope in normal adjustment, magnification is given by $M = \frac{L}{f_o} \times \frac{D}{f_e}$
where $L$ is tube length (10 cm), $f_o$ is objective focal length (2 cm), $f_e$ is eyepiece focal length (5 cm), and $D$ is least distance of distinct vision (25 cm).
$M = \frac{10}{2} \times \frac{25}{5} = 5 \times 5 = 25 = 5^2$
Therefore, $k = 2$

subject: Physics
q: A slanted object $A B$ is placed on one side of convex lens as shown in the diagram. The image is formed on the opposite side. Angle made by the image with principal axis is:
question: A slanted object $A B$ is placed on one side of convex lens as shown in the diagram. The image is formed on the opposite side. Angle made by the image with principal axis is:
solution:
Location of image of $\mathrm{A}:-$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{1}{-30}=\frac{1}{20} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{1}{60} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=60 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{~m}=2\end{aligned}$
Since size of object is small wrt the location hence
$\begin{aligned} & d v=m^2 d u \Rightarrow d v=4 \times 1=4 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & h_i=m h_0 \Rightarrow h_i(d y)=2 \times 2=4 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Angle made with principle axis $=-45^{\circ}$

subject: Physics
q: The size of the images of an object, formed by a thin lens are equal when the object is placed at two different positions 8 cm and 24 cm from the lens. The focal length of the lens is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: The size of the images of an object, formed by a thin lens are equal when the object is placed at two different positions 8 cm and 24 cm from the lens. The focal length of the lens is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
The magnification of a thin lens is given by $m = \frac{f}{f + u}$.
Given that the size of the images is equal for two different object positions, the magnitudes of the magnifications must be equal: $|m_1| = |m_2|$.
For a single lens to produce images of the same size at two different object distances, one image must be real and the other must be virtual.
Let $u_1 = -8$ cm and $u_2 = -24$ cm.
Then, $m_1 = -m_2$ (since one is real and one is virtual).
$\frac{f}{f + u_1} = -\frac{f}{f + u_2}$
$\frac{1}{f - 8} = -\frac{1}{f - 24}$
$f - 24 = -(f - 8)$
$f - 24 = -f + 8$
$2f = 32$
$f = 16$ cm.
The focal length of the lens is 16 cm.

subject: Physics
q: A light ray is incident on a glass slab of thickness $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ and refractive index $\sqrt{2}$ . The angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle for the glass slab with air. The lateral displacement of ray after passing through glass slab is _____ $\mathrm{cm}$. $\left(\right.$ Given $\left.\sin 15^{\circ}=0.25\right)$
question: A light ray is incident on a glass slab of thickness $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ and refractive index $\sqrt{2}$ . The angle of incidence is equal to the critical angle for the glass slab with air. The lateral displacement of ray after passing through glass slab is _____ $\mathrm{cm}$. $\left(\right.$ Given $\left.\sin 15^{\circ}=0.25\right)$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & i=\theta_c \\ & \Rightarrow i=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\mu}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow i=45^{\circ} \end{aligned}$ and according to snell's law $\begin{aligned} & 1 \sin 45^{\circ}=\sqrt{2} \sin \mathrm{r} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=30^{\circ} \end{aligned}$
Lateral displacement $\Delta=\frac{\mathrm{t} \sin (\mathrm{i}-\mathrm{r})}{\cos \mathrm{r}}$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \Delta=\frac{4 \sqrt{3} \times \sin 15^{\circ}}{\cos 30^{\circ}} \\ & \Rightarrow \Delta=2 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The following figure represents two biconvex lenses $L_1$ and $L_2$ having focal length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. The distance between $L_1 \& L_2$ is :
question: The following figure represents two biconvex lenses $L_1$ and $L_2$ having focal length $10 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. The distance between $L_1 \& L_2$ is :
solution: $D=f_1+f_2=25 \mathrm{~cm}$ Paraxial parallel rays pass through focus and ray from focus of convex lens will become parallel

subject: Physics
q: A bi-convex lens has radius of curvature of both the surfaces same as $1 / 6 \mathrm{~cm}$. If this lens is required to be replaced by another convex lens having different radii of curvatures on both sides $\left(R_1 \neq R_2\right)$, without any change in lens power then possible combination of $R_1$ and $R_2$ is :
question: A bi-convex lens has radius of curvature of both the surfaces same as $1 / 6 \mathrm{~cm}$. If this lens is required to be replaced by another convex lens having different radii of curvatures on both sides $\left(R_1 \neq R_2\right)$, without any change in lens power then possible combination of $R_1$ and $R_2$ is :
solution:
This will happen when
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_1}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_2} \\ & (\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{-\mathrm{R}_2}\right)=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}\right) \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: When a beam of white light is allowed to pass through convex lens parallel to principal axis, the different colours of light converge at different point on the principle axis after refraction. This is called :
question: When a beam of white light is allowed to pass through convex lens parallel to principal axis, the different colours of light converge at different point on the principle axis after refraction. This is called :
solution:
Due to difference in refractive index of a material for different wavelengths, the focal length will be different for different colours. This will cause a separation of parallel beam of white light. This phenomenon is known as chromatic aberration.

Polarisation is a property applying to transverse waves that specify the geometrical orientation of the oscillations.

When energy waves (such as light, sound, and various electromagnetic waves) are caused to depart from a straight path due to imperfections in the medium it is called Scattering.

Spherical aberration is present when the outer parts of a lens do not bring light rays into the same focus as the central part.

subject: Physics
q: To get three images of a single object, one should have two plane mirrors at an angle of
question: To get three images of a single object, one should have two plane mirrors at an angle of
solution:
According to Image formula
$\mathrm{n}=\frac{360}{\theta}-1 \Rightarrow 3=\frac{360}{\theta}-1$
$\Rightarrow \frac{360}{\theta}=4 \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{360}{4}=90$

The plot of the image distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msup><mi>v</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mfenced></math> against the object distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msup><mi>μ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mfenced></math> measured from the focus gives a curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>v</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><msup><mi>μ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>225</mn></math>. question: In an experiment with a convex lens. The plot of the image distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msup><mi>v</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mfenced></math> against the object distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msup><mi>μ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></mfenced></math> measured from the focus gives a curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>v</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><msup><mi>μ</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>225</mn></math>. If all the distances are measured in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>. The magnitude of the focal length of the lens is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Using Newton's formula for lenses,

vu=f2 , here, f is focal length of the lens.

Given here, v'μ'=225

Thus, focal length of the lens is f2=225

Or f=15 cm

subject: Physics
q: A parallel beam of light travelling in air (refractive index 1.0) is incident on a convex spherical glass surface of radius of curvature 50 cm. Refractive index of glass is 1.5. The rays converge to a point at a distance $x \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the curvature of the spherical surface. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
question: A parallel beam of light travelling in air (refractive index 1.0) is incident on a convex spherical glass surface of radius of curvature 50 cm. Refractive index of glass is 1.5. The rays converge to a point at a distance $x \mathrm{~cm}$ from the centre of the curvature of the spherical surface. The value of $x$ is $\_\_\_\_$ cm.
solution:
For refraction at a single spherical surface, we use the formula: $\frac{\mu_2}{v} - \frac{\mu_1}{u} = \frac{\mu_2 - \mu_1}{R}$
Given data:
Refractive index of air $\mu_1 = 1.0$
Refractive index of glass $\mu_2 = 1.5$
Radius of curvature $R = +50$ cm (convex surface)
Object is at infinity for a parallel beam, so $u = -\infty$
Substituting the values:
$\frac{1.5}{v} - \frac{1.0}{-\infty} = \frac{1.5 - 1.0}{50}$
$\frac{1.5}{v} - 0 = \frac{0.5}{50}$
$\frac{1.5}{v} = \frac{1}{100}$
$v = 1.5 \times 100 = 150$ cm
The image is formed at a distance of $150$ cm from the pole (vertex) of the spherical surface.
The question asks for the distance $x$ from the centre of curvature.
Distance from centre of curvature $x = |v - R|$
$x = 150 - 50 = 100$ cm

When it is measured in two different liquids having refractive indices <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>  and <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>, it has the focal lengths <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mn>1</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mn>2</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> respectively. The correct relation between the focal lengths is :
question: A thin convex lens made from crown glass <math> <mrow> <mfenced close=")" open="(" separators=","> <mrow> <mi>μ</mi> <mo>=</mo> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </mfenced> </mrow> </math> has focal length <math> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> </math>. When it is measured in two different liquids having refractive indices <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>4</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>  and <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>, it has the focal lengths <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mn>1</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mrow> <mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi></mi><mi>f</mi><mo>⁡</mo> </mrow> <mn>2</mn> </msub> </mrow> </math> respectively. The correct relation between the focal lengths is :
solution:
Let [ 1 R 1 - 1 R 2 ] = x

∴ 1 f = [ 3 2 1 - 1 ] [ 1 R 1 - 1 R 2 ] ⇒ f = 2 x

When in liquid 1

1 f 1 = [ 3 2 4 3 - 1 ] [ 1 R 1 - 1 R 2 ] ⇒ f 1 = 8 x

When in liquid 2

1 f 2 = [ 3 2 5 3 - 1 ] [ 1 R 1 - 1 R 2 ] ⇒ f 2 = - 10 x

subject: Physics
q: A thin convex lens of focal length f is put on a plane mirror as shown in the figure. When an object is kept at a distance a from the lens-mirror combination, its image is formed at a distance a3 in front of the combination. The value of a is:
question: A thin convex lens of focal length f is put on a plane mirror as shown in the figure. When an object is kept at a distance a from the lens-mirror combination, its image is formed at a distance a3 in front of the combination. The value of a is:
solution:
Equivalent power of combination is

Peff=2Plens+Pmirror ...(i)

∵Pmirror =0 for plane mirror and Plens=1f

Also combination behaves as a mirror. So. Peff=1fmirror

In equation (i)

-1fmirror =2f⇒fmirror=-f2

By mirror formula

1fmirror =1u+1v

Here u= -a, v=-a3, fmirror= -f2

⇒-2f=1-a+3-a=-4a

⇒-2f=-4a⇒a=2f

subject: Physics
q: A thin convex lens and a thin concave lens are kept in contact and are co-axial. Which of the following statements is correct for this combination of two lenses ?
question: A thin convex lens and a thin concave lens are kept in contact and are co-axial. Which of the following statements is correct for this combination of two lenses ?
options: Focal length of the lens system will change if the positions of two lenses are interchanged
solution:
The equivalent focal length $F$ of two thin lenses in contact is given by $\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{1}{f_1} + \dfrac{1}{f_2}$

For a convex lens, the focal length is positive, so $f_1 = |f_{\text{convex}}|$

For a concave lens, the focal length is negative, so $f_2 = -|f_{\text{concave}}|$

Substituting these values, we get $\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{convex}}|} - \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{concave}}|}$

If $|f_{\text{convex}}| > |f_{\text{concave}}|$, then $\dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{convex}}|} < \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{concave}}|}$

This implies $\dfrac{1}{F} < 0$, so $F$ is negative.

A negative equivalent focal length indicates that the combination behaves as a diverging or concave lens.

Interchanging the positions of the lenses does not change the equivalent focal length since $\dfrac{1}{f_1} + \dfrac{1}{f_2} = \dfrac{1}{f_2} + \dfrac{1}{f_1}$.

Answer: behaves as concave lens if $|f_{\text{convex}}| > |f_{\text{concave}}|$

subject: Physics
q: A bi convex lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is cut in two identical parts along a plane perpendicular to the principal axis. The power of each lens after cut is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>.
question: A bi convex lens of focal length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is cut in two identical parts along a plane perpendicular to the principal axis. The power of each lens after cut is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>.
solution:
The focal length of the bi-convex lens f can be calculated as follows:

1f=(μ-1)1R-1-R=2μ-1R   ...1

When the lens is cut into two halves, the focal length f' of each part can be calculated as follows:

1f'=(μ-1)1R-1∞= μ-1R   ...2

Hence, the ratio of the powers for both the lenses can be given by

P'P=1f'1f= ff'=μ-1R2μ-1R=12⇒P'= P2   ...3

Substitute the value of the known parameter into equation (3) to calculate the required power.

P'=10 D2= 5 D

subject: Physics
q: A green light is incident from the water to the air - water interface at the critical angle θc. Select the correct statement.
question: A green light is incident from the water to the air - water interface at the critical angle θc. Select the correct statement.
options: The entire spectrum of visible light will come out of the water at an angle of 90<sup>o</sup> to the normal
The spectrum of visible light whose frequency is less than that of green light will come out to the air medium
The spectrum of visible light whose frequency is more than that of green light will come out to the air medium
The entire spectrum of visible light will come out of the water at various angles to the normal
solution:
sin θ c = 1 μ

For greater wavelength (i.e lesser frequency) μ is less So, θ c would be more. So, they will not suffer reflection and come out at angles less than 90 ∘ .
